coredata.dc:title: Sensitive imprinted optical sensor based on mesoporous structure and green nanoparticles for the detection of methamphetamine in plasma and urine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mandani, Sudabe
Rezaei, Behzad
Ensafi, Ali Asghar
coredata.dc:description: Methamphetamine (MA), a psychoactive substance with many medicinal applications in different countries, has destructive impacts on the nervous system and brain and can lead to addiction. The optimal system for MA determination must be able to measure the tiny amount of MA in complex matrixes accurately. In the current work, a simple and biocompatible sensitive optical probe was developed based on molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) technique and by using green CQDs and mesoporous structured imprinting microspheres (SiO2@CQDs@ms-MIPs). CQDs (ФF = 33%) were synthesized via the hydrothermal method using natural chewing gum as carbon source. SiO2 nanoparticles were used as the backup substrate for the placement of CQDs. In spite of biocompatibility, porosity and having high specific area are the unique features of SiO2 nanoparticles. When MA is present, the fluorescence response of MIPs enhances. This is caused by the passivation and adjustment of active clusters that are present on the surface of CQDs. By this optical sensor, the favorable linear dynamic range (5.0–250 μM) and the detection limit (1.6 μM) were obtained. The applicability of the advanced sensor was studied in real samples such as human urine and human blood plasma. Acceptable results were obtained and recovery amounts were in the 92–110% interval.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green carbon dots
Natural chewing gum
Mesoporous nanoparticles
Optical sensor
Methamphetamine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from natural chewing gum
pH Influence on the fluorescence signal
Influence of MIPs amount on the fluorescence signal

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry and Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We wish to thank the Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry and Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) and Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for their patronage and help in this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the general performance of the offered system.
(A) UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelength.
(A) SEM image of SiO2@QDs. (B) TEM image of SiO2@QDs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of SiO2 and SiO2@QDs.
(A) AFM image of SiO2 nanoparticles. (B) AFM image of SiO2@QDs nanoparticles. (C) AFM image of SiO2@QDs nanoparticles, all AFM images were obtained in the contact mode with the 1 μm × 1 μm window.
(A) FESEM image of CQDs@SiO2@ms-MIPs. (B) FESEM image of CQDs@SiO2@ms-NIPs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs@SiO2@ms-MIPs and FT-IR spectrum of CQDs@SiO2@ms-NIPs.
Regeneration of probe versus a number of experiments at the optimal situations.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) CQDs@SiO2@ms-MIPs and (B) CQDs@SiO2@ms-NIPs, at the optimum situations in the presence of different concentrations of MA as 0.0, 5, 25, 20.0, 50, 75.0, 100, 150.0, 175.0, 200.0, 225.0, 220.0 and 250.0 μM.
Calibration curve for MA at the optimum situations in the presence of different concentrations of MA as 0.0, 5, 25, 20.0, 50, 75.0, 100, 150.0, 175.0, 200.0, 225.0, 220.0 and 250.0 μM.
The answers of CQDs, CQDs@SiO2, CQDs@SiO2@ms-NIPs, and CQDs@SiO2@ms-MIPs to 0.5 mmol/L various species in the existence of 50.0 μM MA at the optimum situations.
Recovery test results in the plasma and urine samples.
Comparisons of the experimental results obtained by this method and HPLC for determination of MA.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ttable = 3.8 (n = 3, 98%), in all results, texp ˂ ttable, so there is no significant difference between the offered method and HPLC for MA determination.

head.ce:title: Sensitive imprinted optical sensor based on mesoporous structure and green nanoparticles for the detection of methamphetamine in plasma and urine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methamphetamine (MA), a psychoactive substance with many medicinal applications in different countries, has destructive impacts on the nervous system and brain and can lead to addiction. The optimal system for MA determination must be able to measure the tiny amount of MA in complex matrixes accurately. In the current work, a simple and biocompatible sensitive optical probe was developed based on molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) technique and by using green CQDs and mesoporous structured imprinting microspheres (SiO2@CQDs@ms-MIPs). CQDs (ФF = 33%) were synthesized via the hydrothermal method using natural chewing gum as carbon source. SiO2 nanoparticles were used as the backup substrate for the placement of CQDs. In spite of biocompatibility, porosity and having high specific area are the unique features of SiO2 nanoparticles. When MA is present, the fluorescence response of MIPs enhances. This is caused by the passivation and adjustment of active clusters that are present on the surface of CQDs. By this optical sensor, the favorable linear dynamic range (5.0–250 μM) and the detection limit (1.6 μM) were obtained. The applicability of the advanced sensor was studied in real samples such as human urine and human blood plasma. Acceptable results were obtained and recovery amounts were in the 92–110% interval.

ce:section.ce:para: Methamphetamine, a persuasive nervous stimulus, is used for the treatment of patients with obesity and alcohol dependency [1]. In spite of the above-mentioned advantages, it has a lot of destructive side effects, such as increasing blood pressure and heartbeat and affecting body temperature balance, mood, and attention [2]. The main destructive effect of methamphetamine is an addiction. So, its easy synthesis and obtainability have produced many problems for human societies [3,4]. Accordingly, the preceding remarks highlight the importance of determining MA [5]. Various determination and quantification methods have been proposed for the measurement of MA in different biological samples such as hair, saliva, urine and blood fluid [6]. However, given that most of these methods are financially impractical, time consuming, and, in some cases, too complex, they are not suitable [7]. Recently, by using biocompatible materials, simple and cheap detection systems with high sensitivity and selectivity have been developed. Compared to other methods, optical procedures have some advantages such as low cost, simple application, high sensitivity, and fast response to the determination of the quantity of a wide range of materials. In optical sensors, the base of operation is the variation in several parameters like fluorescence intensity, UV–Vis absorption, surface plasmon resonance, Raman and chemiluminescence signals [8]. Among these, fluorescence methods have better characteristics such as simplicity, high sensitivity, and cost efficiency. Thus, they are preferred for the determination of trace amounts of a wide range of materials [9].

Nowadays, a new class of fluorophores, called quantum dots (QDs), have replaced other organic dyes which were conventionally used as labels in fluorescence sensing systems [10,11]. Optical probes, working on QDs properties, are widely used for the detection of various materials such as metals, small molecules, pollutants, cells, biomacromolecules. Given that QDs are toxic and contain heavy metals, their application has been eliminated and green types of QDs are offered for different applications, especially for biological applications. CQDs are new nanoparticles in the size range of 2–10 nm [12] and with exceptional properties such as high stability, low toxicity, simple synthesis, and being eco-friendly. Hence, CQDs are extensively used in different fields such as catalysis [13] medicine [14] and development of detection devices [15]. Various approaches such as hydrothermal [16,17], microwave [18], electrochemical oxidation, ultrasonic [19,20] and different chemical methods have been developed for the formation of CQDs [21]. Synthetic antibodies, known as MIP, have been synthesized by molecular imprinting technique. After removing template molecules from the polymer by elution, free obtained sites, which are proportional to the shape and the size of the chosen template molecules, are formed on the polymer structure [22,23]. The obtained structure works similar to key-lock systems. MIPs have high attraction and selectivity for the template molecule [24]. To arrange the structure of MIPs, precipitation polymerization, bulk polymerization, suspension polymerization, multi-step swelling polymerization, and sol-gel polymerization synthesis methods were used [25]. Compared to other methods, the sol-gel technique has many benefits such as less energy consumption and better homogeneity, versatility, and simplicity [26]. In recent years, by combining the sensitivity of fluorescence methods, the unique features of green carbon quantum dots and the advantages of the MIPs nanocomposites, various detection systems with high selectivity and pleasant detectability have been developed. Because of the limitations of old-fashioned MIP syntheses, such as partial template removal, minor binding ability, low affinity, and unbalanced material shapes, today's novel MIPs are synthesized on the surface of various matrixes such as magnetic substances [27,28] silica nanoparticles, polymers [29], nanostructures [30] and other resources. The imprinting of core-shell MIPs surface has a lot of interesting properties such as better [31] spreading, more site availability, better mass transfer and template removal [29,32]. Silica nanoparticles, due to their good features and high biocompatibility, are the current assistant material. In the current work, via the sol-gel polymerization mechanism and using green CQDs and mesoporous silica nanoparticles, a modest and sensitive surface imprinting fluorescence sensor was developed for the quantification of MA in biological fluids.

N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), sodium hydroxide, N-cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich CO. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), ammonia solution (25%) and ethanol (EtOH) (99.6%) were supplied from Merck CO. Natural chewing gum was purchased from a local market in Isfahan, Iran. Methamphetamine was obtained from the Antinarcotics Police of Isfahan, Iran. All materials were in analytical rank and were used without any modifications.

Double beam Jasco 570 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was applied for the registration of all absorption spectra. The fluorescence spectra were attained by a Jasco FP-750 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan). Philips XL30 field effect-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) operating at 25 kV (the Netherlands) and Philips CM-30 300 kV transition electron microscopy (TEM) operating at 300 kV were used for the characterization of nanoparticles. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were demonstrated by Bruker D8/Advance X-ray diffractometer (Germany). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern ZEN 3600 UK) was applied for estimating the size spreading of CQDs, SiO2 nanoparticles, and CQDs@SiO2@ms-MIP. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analyses were done by Burker AFM microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from natural chewing gum
ce:section.ce:para: Natural chewing gum was applied as the origin of carbon for the synthesis of CQDs via the hydrothermal method. This gum is solids consisting of mixtures of polysaccharides (carbohydrates) which are either water-soluble or absorb water and swell up to form a gel or jelly when placed in water. That can serve as suitable carbon precursors, the resultant CDs exhibit a high QY of up to 30%, which is far superior to the most values of waste-derived CDs [33]. 5% W/V solution of chewing gum was prepared and transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave and heated in the oven at 200 °C for 720 min. Then, the autoclave cooled at room temperature. To remove large particles, the produced brownish suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and, then, passed through a syringe filter 0.45 μM. The CQDs solution was kept in a dark container and stored at 4 °C. CQDs were produced through the carbonization of the sugar units of the chewing gum [31] such as galactose, glucose, rhamnose, glucuronic acid, and arabinose.

15 mL of EtOH, 5 mL of DI water and ammonia solution were mixed and stirred to homogeneity. Then, 2.5 mL of TEOS and 10 mL of EtOH were added slowly. Finally, 2.5 mL of APTES was added and the solution was magnetically stirred overnight to complete the reaction. The product was a white precipitate containing amino-functionalized SiO2 nanoparticles.

Via the FT-IR spectra, the existence of a carboxyl functional group on the surface of CQDs was confirmed. The following method was used to connect the CQDs to the amino-functional group at the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles. At first, 5 mg of CQDs was added to 1 mL of PBS (1 mM) with pH = 7.4 that contained 15 mg of EDC and the mixture was stirred for 3 min. Then, 5 mg of NHS was added to the container and was stirred for 1 h. Finally, 20 mg of SiO2-NH2 nanoparticles was added and the blend was stirred in dark conditions for 12 h. The centrifuged precipitate was dispersed in the PBS solution with pH = 7.4 and stored at 4 °C.

5 mL of SiO2@CQDs was well dispersed ultrasonically in 15 mL of DI water for 10 min. Then, 80 μL of APTES and 10 mg of MA were added. When stirring the solution, we poured 0.8 mL of CTAB (0.2 M) and 0.1 mL of NaOH (0.2 M) to the container. After 30 min, 100 μL of TEOS and 100 μL of ammonia solution were added. The blend was stirred for 12 h at ambient temperature in dark conditions. The precipitate was sometimes washed with 8:2 EtOH/HCl (8:2 V/V) solution to remove CTAB and template molecules and then it dried in vacuum at 40 °C for 2 h. As the reference, NIPs (SiO2@CQDs@ms-NIPs) were synthesized in the same manner but MA was not added to the mixture.

By ultrasonic vibration, 2 mg of MIPs was dispersed in 4 mL of PBS with pH = 7.4. To determine the fluorescence signals, the excitation wavelength was fixed at 330 nm and scanning was done in the 350–600 nm region. In the presence and absence of different concentrations of MA in the standard solution, F and F0 were recorded and FF0 was calculated as an analytical parameter.

All urine and blood plasma samples of human were obtained from Khorshid Health Center, (Isfahan, Iran). At first, the applicability of the developed sensor was studied by the spiked method in human plasma samples. Various amounts of MA standard solutions were mixed with 2.0 mL of a plasma sample. Then, for the precipitation of plasma proteins, 2 mL of 10% (W/V) trichloroacetic acid was added to the solutions and was centrifuged for 15 min at 6000 rpm. The upper phase was separated and diluted with the phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4) ten times. For the quantification of MA, 200 μL of this sample was added to the 2 mL of the prepared suspension which included 1 mg/L of MIPs at pH = 7.4. As well, 200 μL of urine sample was added to 2 mL of the suspension. Quantities of MA in all samples were estimated by the fluorescence method.

The performance of the offered system is illustrated in the schematic diagram shown in Fig. 1 . UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, which are displayed in Fig. 2A, show a sharp peak at 268 nm. This peak is most probably originated by π to π* transition of CC conjugated bonds. Fluorescence spectra of CQDs were studied in various excitation wavelengths and the results are provided in Fig. 2B. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs depends on the excitation wavelength. The maximum intensity of fluorescence was followed in the excitation wavelength of 330; thus, 330 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength. The fluorescence signal of CQDs, CQDs@SiO2, and CQDs@SiO2@MIPs are displayed in Fig. S1. The observed redshifts are due to an increase in the size of nanoparticles.

To find the average size of CQDs particles, DLS technique was used. The particle size distribution was in the 3–8 nm range (Fig. S2). Fig. S3A shows the TEM analysis of CQDs, as can be seen, the shape of the nanoparticles is spherical and the average size is 4 nm, approximately. Also, the XRD pattern of CQDs, displayed in Fig. S3B, shows a wide spectrum at 2Ө = 20.75 that corresponds to the diffraction plane (002). This is indicative of the amorphous structure of the CQDs. In the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, the peaks at 3415, 2931, and 1596 corresponded to OH, CH, and COO– stretching vibrations, respectively. Also, the peaks observed at 1522 cm− 1 and 1419 cm− 1 demonstrate HOH bending and CC stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 1118 cm− 1 and 1074 cm− 1 are associated with the CC, CO and COC bending vibrations, respectively (S4).

As well, the size distribution of SiO2 nanoparticles is in the 59–69 nm interval, which was estimated by DLS analysis (Fig. S5). The XRD patterns of SiO2 nanoparticles, provided in Fig. S6, include a broad peak around 2Ө = 27.4 which corresponds to diffraction plane (101) and confirms the amorphous structure of the SiO2 nanoparticles. Also, this pattern evidently confirms the absence of any crystalline nanostructure. The morphology of SiO2 nanoparticles was studied by FESEM analysis and, as can be seen, this substance is spherical in shape, (Fig. 3A). TEM image of SiO2@QDs is depicted in Fig. 3B. The red arrow is pointing to the regions that are related to linked QDs to the SiO2 nanoparticles. In the FT-IR of SiO2, the peaks at 3436 cm− 1 and 1639 cm− 1 are related to the NH stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1097 cm− 1, 952 cm− 1 and 798 cm− 1 correspond to SiOSi, SiOH, and SiO stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 466 cm− 1 is related to the bending vibration of SiOSi. In the FTIR spectrum of SiO2@QDs, a peak that appeared at 1411 cm− is related to the stretching vibration of CN acylamino group and confirms the grafting of CQDs on SiO2 surface, (Fig. 3C). AFM results of SiO2 and SiO2@QDs are shown in Fig. 4 . In Fig. 4A the 3D structure of SiO2 nanoparticles confirms the spherical shape of them. In Fig. 4B and C, the 3D structure of SiO2 nanoparticles that linked to QDs is illustrated. The appearance of tip structures on the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles represents the connection between QDs and SiO2 nanoparticles.

The morphologies of CQDs@SiO2@ms@MIPs and CQDs@SiO2@ms@NIPs were studied by FESEM analysis (Fig. 5A and B). As can be seen, these substances are spherical in shape. Polymerization is the cause of the increase observed in the size of MIPs and NIPs, compared with SiO2 nanoparticles.

Also, the FT-IR spectra of CQDs@SiO2@ms@MIPs and CQDs@SiO2@ms@NIPs are shown in Fig. 5C. In the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, the peaks at 3415, 2931, and 1596 correspond to OH, CH, and COO– stretching vibrations, respectively. Also, the peaks observed at 1522 cm− 1 and 1419 cm− 1 demonstrate HOH bending and CC stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 1118 cm− 1 and 1074 cm− 1 are associated with the CC, CO, and COC bending vibrations, respectively. The FT-IR spectra for MIPs and NIPs have the same structure because a thin layer of MIPs is formed on the SiO2@QDs surface.

To form an imprinting silica layer on the surface of SiO2@QDs, CTAB, APTES, and TEOS which acted as a surfactant, functional monomer, and cross linker, respectively were used. MA was the template molecule and NH3.H2O acted as the catalyst. Then, CTAB and template molecules were removed and SiO2@QDs@ms-MIPs with specific imprinting cavities and mesoporous structures were created. As mentioned above, the main advantages of this system were the rapid diffusion of template molecules, a decrease in the toxicity of QDs, and the high accessibility of binding sites. The obtained results showed that, by the addition of MA, fluorescence intensity is enhanced. While the fluorescence properties of CQDs are not entirely identified, two sources were stated for the fluorescence emission of CQDs. The presence of sp2 clusters is one of them and its π states control this origin [30]. It should be noted that the bandgap of σ to σ* states affects the electronic levels of π to π * from the sp2 cluster. Radiative recombinant of hole-electron pairs which exit in sp2 clusters creates fluorescence emission. The presence of surface state is another reason for the decrease of fluorescence in CQDs. The surface state affects the fluorescence behavior of CQDs more than the intrinsic central state. Some traps which are present on the surface of CQDs, defect states, and non-radiative relaxation processes can reduce fluorescence intensity by dropping the excited electrons to the ground state without producing any photon. Passivation, modification of the surface of CQDs, and the reduction of some groups such as alkylamines, carbonyl, and epoxy groups which are connected to the CDs are some processes performed to enhance fluorescence intensity of CQDs [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH Influence on the fluorescence signal
ce:section.ce:para: For estimation, the optimum pH for the probe application, PBS solutions with a pH in the interval of 2.0–10.0 were prepared. Then, fluorescence enhancement was recorded for 50 μM of MA in a solution containing 1 mg of MIPs in 2 mL PBS (Fig. S7A). According to the results, at pH = 7.4, the maximum fluorescence enhancement was observed. Hence, pH = 7.4 was selected as the optimal amount. At the basic pH, silica nanoparticles were ionized and deformed. In conditions with pH < 7.4, the interaction between MA and nanoparticles decreased. This is due to the protonation of functional monomers (APTES).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of MIPs amount on the fluorescence signal
ce:section.ce:para: For this reason, PBS (pH = 7.4) solutions containing MIPs in the range of 200–700 mg/L were prepared. Fluorescence intensity in the presence of 50 μM MA was recorded at room temperature. In the presence of 500 mg/L of MIPs, the maximum enhancement was recorded. However, in the presence of higher amounts of imprinted polymer, self-quenching happened. Thus, 500 mg/L was selected as the optimum amount of MIPs (Fig. S7B).

At this stage, sufficient time for the adsorption of MA on MIPs was estimated. Fluorescence changes in the PBS solution (pH = 7.4), containing 500 mg/L MIPs and 50 μM MA, were recorded at room temperature. For 30 s, an enhancement was observed and, then, fluorescence intensity was constant. Therefore, 30 s was considered as the optimal time in all processes (Fig. S7C).

In this step, the most suitable temperature for receiving the most proper response of the sensor to MA was estimated. Fluorescence changes in the PBS solution (pH = 7.4), containing 500 mg L MIPs and 50 μM MA, were recorded at different temperatures. For 30 s, a maximum enhancement was observed at room temperature. Therefore, 25 °C was considered as the optimal temperature in all processes (Fig. S7D).

To investigate the possibility of reusing the sensor, the following was done: fluorescence enhancement for the solution contains 500 mg/L of MIPs and 50 μM MA, was recorded at the optimum condition. Then, the sensor was washed three times with EtOH/HCl (8:2 V/V) and the above process was repeated. The observed results showed that, after 5 times, the response of the probe interrupted. Hence, the sensor can be used up to 4 times for detection (Fig. 6 ).

According to Fig. 7A, MA addition enhances the fluorescence signal of the CQDs@SiO2@ms-MIPs. As a blank, the influence of MA addition on the fluorescence intensity of the non-imprinted polymers, SiO2@CQDs@ms-NIPs nanocomposite, was investigated and the results can be seen in Fig. 7B. The comparison between Fig. 7A and Fig. 7B clearly shows that the enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of MIPs was greatly more than NIPs, which is due to the presence of cavities that are designed for MA in the nanocomposite structure of MIPs. The selectivity of SiO2@CQDs@ms-MIPs is due to the presence of holes which are compatible with the shape, size, and configuration of the molecule which is under investigation on the surface of the nanocomposite. Under optimized conditions, probe responses to different concentrations of MA were recorded. Excellent linearity with the correlation coefficient of R2 = 0. 9904 was obtained. As can be seen in Fig. 8 , a satisfactory limit of detection (LOD = 1.6 μM), which is consistent with the goal of the present study (i.e., measuring trace amounts of MA), is obtained.

Selectivity, as one of the most important analytical characteristics of a detection system, refers to the ability of the system to determine a particular target in a complex environment and at the presence of other analytes, without any interference. To evaluate this parameter for the developed sensor at optimal conditions, the response of SiO2@CQDs@ms-MIIPs to various spices such as glycine, alanine, caffeine, morphine, dopamine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, Cl−, Ca+2, and K+ was determined. The results reported in Fig. 9 show that the selectivity of SiO2@CQDs@ms-MIPs for methamphetamine is much higher than that for SiO2@CQDs@ms-NIPs, SiO2@QDs, and CQDs.

Recovery and standard methods were used to evaluate the performance of the probe in biological fluids such as human urine and blood plasma samples. To achieve this aim, different concertations of MA in human plasma and human urine samples were studied. The results of the determination are shown in Table 1 . The estimated recoveries of MA were in the 92–110% intervals. These results show that the settled probe is suitable for analyzing trace amounts of MA in complex samples. The results of the comparison of this method with HPLC are reported in Table 2 and they show no significant differences.

The obtained figures of merit for the developed sensor were compared with the corresponding figures of some other methods used for MA quantification (Table. S1). The limit of quantification and the linear dynamic range of this probe are comparable and, in some cases, better. In addition, the developed probe is not expensive and has some advantages such as fast response, biocompatibility, applicability, and availability.

In the traditional methods for the synthesis of MIPs, QDs were encapsulated by polymerization with cross linkers, therefore, QDs were far from the surface of nanoparticles. Because of the above-mentioned reason, the interactive sites between the target and probe were less accessible and this could reduce the sensitivity of the method. Recently, through the surface imprinting process, the layer of MIPs forms on the surface of CQDs. In addition, the formation of the mesoporous structure increases the penetration and availability of active sites and, accordingly, improves the applicability of the developed probe. In the current work, through the sol-gel core-shell method and using green CQDs, which were produced via natural chewing gum, a simple and biocompatible MA probe was developed based on SiO2@QDs@ms-MIPs. The developed sensor works based on enhancing fluorescence intensity in the presence of MA. The recovery and standard analysis results are indicative of the successful performance of the sensor in complex fluids such as human urine and human blood plasma samples. In addition, this sensor can detect tiny amounts of MA (LOD = 1.6 μM) and works selectively in the presence of various substances that may exist in urine and blood plasma. Finally, it can be argued that the developed sensor, having proper figures of merit and good dispersion in aqueous environment is suitable for the detection of MA in bio environments.
Sudabe Mandani was responsible for the design, synthesis, characterization of compounds and data analysis.
Prof. Behzad Rezaei was the corresponding author, and he was responsible for the direction of experiments, writing review & editing, and supervision.
Prof. Ali Asghar Ensafi was the advisor, and he was responsible for the direction of experiment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We wish to thank the Center of Excellence in Sensor and Green Chemistry and Research Council of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) and Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for their patronage and help in this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118077.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MDMA, methamphetamine, and CYP2D6 pharmacogenetics: what is clinically relevant?
Front. Genet.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. De La Torre, S. Yubero-Lahoz, R. Pardo-Lozano, M. Farré, MDMA, methamphetamine, and CYP2D6 pharmacogenetics: what is clinically relevant?, Frontiers in genetics, 3(2012) 235.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and simultaneous detection of methamphetamine and dimethyl sulfone in crystalline methamphetamine seizures by fast gas chromatography
Forensic Toxicology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Inoue, K. Kuwayama, Y.T. Iwata, T. Kanamori, K. Tsujikawa, H. Miyaguchi, Simple and simultaneous detection of methamphetamine and dimethyl sulfone in crystalline methamphetamine seizures by fast gas chromatography, Forensic Toxicology, 26(2008) 19-22.

Forensic Sci. Int.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Point-of-use detection of amphetamine-type stimulants with host-molecule-functionalized organic transistors
Chem

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel fluorescence nanosensor based on 1, 8-naphthalimide-thiophene doped silica nanoparticles, and its application to the determination of methamphetamine
Sensors Actuators B Chem.
